The annual report to the 31st March, 1888, submitted to the meeting held on the 28th June, 1888, stated as follows: Another year has passed without any improvement having- taken place in the returns from the mining shares held by the company; indeed, the twelve months ending 31st March last show the smallest income of any since the formation of the company. Had it not been for the interest on homo investments the revenue would not have sufficed to meet the very moderate working expenses, only two of the mines in which the company is interested having paid dividends, whilst the calls have been comparatively heavy. Under those circumstances the Board have, after due consideration, decided on recommending the gradual and voluntary liquidation of the co;n| any. By adopting this course an immediate return of about 4s. per share could be made to the shareholders, whilst keeping in hand sufficient funds to pay calls on promising shares pending a favourable opportunity for disposing of them. The realisable value of the colonial mining shares is most uncertain, the quotations being in many cases nominal, whilst odd lots of shares are difficult to dispose of ; the directors cannot hold out much hopes of these producing any considerable amount. An extraordinary general meeting has Vieen called to give effect to the foregoing recommen dation, and will Ire held immediately after the ordinary meeting. Rkedt Cbekk. Special Survey ov 20,000 Acres. Tungkillo Hundred . The year 1880 has been the driest of any since the colony was established, the rainfall during the past three years averaged only 13 inches as against 21 inches in previous yean . The company s tenants are among those whose crops have suffered greatly, and as they had only a short wheat crop in 1885, their resources have been considerably impaired; they have also suffered heavily by losses of horses, cattle and sheep, in consequence of the drought; in view of these untoward circumstances concessions have been made to the tenants by way of relief in the arrears of rents, to the amount of ú190 7s. 6d. There have been good rainfalls since April last, the farmers are therefore again full of hope, and should rext season turn out well, their wsition will l egreatly improved. The value of wool having increased, is of course a great satisfaction and is some set-off against the general depression. The shareholders will remember that after 1858, when mining operations ceased at Tungkillo, the survey was occupied as a sheep-run until 1808, when the survey was divided into farms and let on leases for 21 years expiring 1889 ; the consideration of what is liest to be done when these leases expire is receiving due attention from Sir Samuel Davenport, and the directors of course will be geeatly guided by his judgment and experience. The annual report to the 31st December, 1887, submitted to the meeting held on the 26th June, 1888, stated as follows : The directors continue to receive from time to time encouraging accounts from the manager, and in a letter dated the 10th of April he expresses a well-founded hope that the mine will be in a position to require stamping machinery at no distant date. The forfeiture of vendor s shares credited as paid up to the extent of 10s. which appears in the balance sheet, was mainly brought about by the vendor s sudden death, and bv the failure of his legal personal representative, through want of means, to pay outstanding calls. The vendor has made default, not only in omitting to pay calls, hut also in having failed to pay to the lessors moneys for which he was responsible ; the result of which was that this company has bocn held liable U make certain payments to them which have been satisfied bv a payment in cash of ú1 ,000, and by the allotment of 10,425 fully paid-up shares in the company, being part of the vendor s forfeited shares. The company have, therefore, at their disposal for re-isBue, when opportunity arises, 31,175 shares, the proceeds of which will form in the future a substantial working capital. Your directors regret to state that, they have experienced great difficulty in recovering from other shareholders in England and elsewhere, outstanding calls, which fact, coupled with the forfeiture of so many shares, has rendered the company exceedingly short of available funds with which to continue their operations as originally contemplated. It is essential, in the opinion of your directors, if the mine is to lw devoknied with rapidity and efHciencv, that additional moneys should tic raised, either by the issue of delwntures or preference shares, or in any other manner which may be determined by the shareholders ; and at an early date your directors will submit a scheme fully dealing with the subject. The following are extracts from the prospectus : The mining claims, as stated by the manager, comprise: A tunnel right 3,000 feet in length by 1,600 feet in width. Two claims 1,500 feet by 600 feet each on the Monumental Lode, and seven tunnel lodes know-n as lodes Nos. 1, 2, 3, group: Doyle, Nob. 12, Iff, and 17, aggre gating 13,500 feet of lodes, of which 3,000 feet by GOO are surface locations, and 10,500 feet are tunnel locations, cut and imssed through by the tunnel which has l een driven into the mountain a distance of 860 feot at right angles to the lodes. A most favourable concession, and invaluable for extensive mining operations, as many more lodes may reasonably lie exacted to te found in the mountain still ahead of the face of the tunnel. Wall Street, New York, that with only the present ten stamps in operation 1,3-11 tons of ore were run through in three months, with the following result : For the first 1,000 tons an average of ú 14 l s.per ton, and, as previously referred to, the ore became richer as the work progressed, and the next 311 tons averaged ú 10 5s. per ton, yielding a total of over 1 20,000 in bullion, giving a net profit of ú15,000. This magnificent result was achieved, not from samples selected for mere assay purposes, but from actual mill runs, with only the altove-inentioned tt-n stamps, and from ore taken out from fifteen veins, cut through in process of opening up, investigating, and developing the mine. With forty stamps at work, crushing at tbe same rate as the ten stamps at present on the property, and yielding a return of ú14 10s. per ton, profits would be ú20,000 a mouth, or equal to a dividend on the capital promised to be called up, of over 100 per cent. jer annum. The purchase consideration is ú200,000, payable as to ú70,000 in fully paid-up shares, ú0,000 in cash, and as to the remainder, in shares, debentures, or cash at the option of the directors, which sum includes the payment of the preliminary expenses up to the allotment of shares. The letters of allotment were issued on the 27th January, 1888, application having been received for about 60,000 shares, which have been allotted. The consideration for the property was ú200,000 payablo in fully-paid shares. The following are extracts from the prospectus : The lode which extends at surface for a length of 2,500 ft., is already o cned up to the THE MIXING . 163 GOLD FIELDS OF URUGUAY extent of l. VH ft. nt numerous points, and it shows a most unusual width, in some places as much as 300 ft., and presents at several places the appearance of a large quarry rather than that of an ordinary fold lode. The lode has also l een opened up by a tunnel and other works to a depth of 70 ft., where it shows a width of 150 ft., with the foot wall not yet reached. These developments have exposed immense Ixxlies of ore ready for extraction by quarrying in o en. benches. The reserves of the 1,000 ft. of reef available without the aid of hoisting or pumping machinery are estimated to be 1,500,000 tons, of the net value of ú2,400,000. This very large quantity of auriferous ore already tested or opened out promises an immediate output of several hundreds of tons daily, and a constant supply for many years. Among the numerous trial crushmgs of the ore which have been made at various times at a small neighbouring mil , 29 tons of ore yielded 07 ounces, 04 tons of ore yielded 103 ounces, aud 210 tons of ore yielded 257 ounces, being equal to the average of 1 ounce 7 dwts. of gold per ton of ore. A mill crushing 38 tons taken indiscriminately from various points along the whole length of the 1,000 ft., including the low grade and doubtful ore, showed that the lode contained do average of 12 dols. 70c. of gold per ton, and it is intended to work the ore the entire width of the whole lode at a cost estimated not to exceed 4 dols. per ton. In order to avoid delay, the company has already given orders to Messrs. Fraser and Chalmers, of Chicago, for one of their improved steam mills to work 40 stamps, with sufficient power to ruu additional 20 stamps, and the machinery is to finished within seven weeks. This mill will he erected in the immediate vicinity of the mine, but it is intended hereafter to build an additional mill of 100 stamps on the site above referred to, capable of treating 2oo tons of ore daily. With 100 stamps alone, and assuming 270 working days only, it is estimated that St.ooo tons could be crushed, with a profit of ú86,400 per annum. The mine should readily and constantly supply such a mill, and it is believed that the extensive reserves may warrant the iiumlier of stamps being very considerably increased. The property is held under the Government Mining Laws, subject to a Government tax of . 5 per cent, on the net profits derived from the working of the mine. Powers have been taken to enable the company to purchase or deal with any other mines in the Republic of Uruguay. text: WAIHI GOLD an experimental furnace, and produced gold and silver of the value of ú344 8s. 9d. or at the rate of about ú23 13s. per ton of ore. Several parcels were sent for trial to California, to Swansea, and to London. The Califokxiav Results in gold an l siver were at the rate of 215 dollars 2 cento per ton, or efjual to ú13 per ton. The Swansea Resclts gave upwards of ú14 per ton. I.okdox Result . Upwards of 17 tons were sent in February last to Messrs. Johnson, Matthey, and Co., assayers to the Bank of England, and were found to contain 9 ozs. of gold, and upwards of 54 ozs. of silver to the ton of ore, and to be of the value of about ú50 per ton, A further shipment of 38 tons taken from the heap of 200 tons of ore referred to in Mr. Bayldon s report, has just been sent to Messrs. Johnson, Matthey, and Co. October, 1967 . It was divided into three classes, 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and the results were as follows : 1st class- Gold, 0 ozs. 1 dwt. Silver, 40 ozs. 10 dwt. per ton, of the value of about ú45 per ton, 2nd class divided into two parts 1st portion, gold, 3 ozb. 5 dwt. Silver, 17 ozs. 10 dwt. per ton, of the value of aliout ú10 i er ton. 2nd portion, gold 3 ozs. 12 dwt. Silver, 18 ozs. per ton, of the value of almut ú17 lis. er ton. 3rd class- Gold, 5 ozr. 7 dwt. Silver, 9 oz. per ton, of the value of about ú23 per ton. Prom the Rosemont Reef in the Rosemont Section of the property, eighty -one tons, being the whole of the ore broken from this lode over a distance of 180 feet, were sent to a temporary smelting furnace at the Thames, and gave a result in gold and silver of ú810 10s. 9cL, or equal to ú10 in;r ton. With the mines are highly valuable prior rights to take water from the adjacent river, which will give to the property a large head of WHter for jjower and other purposes all tlrf year round. There is also a cheap and abundant supply of tiinlwr for mining purposes and fuel in the forests close to the mines. Draiwaok. The company s property includes the whole of the hill known as the Rosemont and Union, the trigonometrical station on which is 312 feet nUwe the river level. Several thousands of pounds have been s| ent in open in cr up, driving1 upon and proving the reefs in this hill, and the drives thus made from different points of the hill have completely drained the lodes. A still lower level will l driven from the site of the reduction works, and be the natural outlet for the mines. This level will afford the cheapest and most rapid means for mining development, and provide at the minimum of cost and without pumping an enormous quantity of ore more tlian can be dealt with for many years to come. Some of the quartz broken from the Union Lode was tried by the ordinary wet crushing mill process in use in New Zealand, when it was proved, that by that process only a small IK-reentage of the gold contained in the ore was saved, but now after careful and exhaustive personal enquiry made in Australia, in Kn gland, and in the principal mining1 centres in America for tho l est gold-saving processes, satisfactory results have been obtained, aud suitable machinery will l e adopted. The cost of thin machinery and of the further development of the property upon a scale equal to its great importance, is l eyond the means of the present proprietors, and they have therefore resolved to offer a part of the shares to the public. From the numerous and conplete trials of the ore referred to, it is established that the mines contain quartz of uncommon richness; the erection of the machinery will makes these properties forthwith dividend paving. The proposed machinery will not only enable the company to give a regular output of bullion from their own mine but, having the first water rights adjacent to the mines, it is believed will give the company a complete monojwly of the water privilege, and secure to them a large and profitable business for the neighbouring mines. This company was registered on the 21st January, 1882, to acquire 1,800 acres of land situate at Wassau, on the West Coast of Africa, and to work the gold mines thereon. The authorised capital is ú100,000 in shares of ú1 each, the whole of which lias been issued and fully-paid. The consideration for the property was ú75,000, payable ú33,000 in fully-paid shares and ú41,700 in cash. Annual reports are issued to the 31st December, and meetings are held in April. The transfer form used is common, and the fee 2s. 6d. per deed. No dividend has vet been paid. Prices. Highest, 1887, 9s. 6d. Lowest, 1887, 5s. Latest, 1888, 10s. For the years ending December, 1883, there was a debit balance of ú5,617 2s. 3d. 1884, ú5,226 14b. 8d. 1885, ú2,992 17s. 7d. 1886, ú3,080 12s. Id. making a total of ú16,917 6s. 7d. of expenditure of capital over and above the gold extracted from the commencement of the development of the mine up to the 31st December, 1886. To the 31st December, 1887, the profit and loss account for the year, showed a credit balance of ú1,053 14s. 6d. ANNUAL REPORT. The annual report to the 31st December, 1887, submitted to the meeting held on the 18th April, 1888, stated as follows : In presenting to the shareholders a statement of accounts for the year ending 31st Decern tier, 1887, the directors are able for the first time to report, that the remittances of gold have left a small balaneo over and alxjve the total expenditure. The ore passed through tho mill was 2,71ft tons as against 2,320 tons for 188ti, and the average yield of gold per ton has lxjen fully maintained. In the retxirt to the shareholders for the year 188fl, reference was made to a change in the supervision of the mine substituting native for European officers , with the periodical superin tendence of a duly qualified European. This change has, up to the present time, t en found to work satisfactorily, and has resulted in a considerable saving in the cost of management. The directors have great confidence in Mr. Sam, the resident manager of the mines, whose skill, aptitude, and energy, give every satisfaction. They have now to report an agreement having been entered into with Mr. L. F. Gowans for a period of three years, as general superintendent of the company s property, to spend six months in each year in visiting the mines, and t confer with and advise the manager as to the development and general working of the property. For this purtM se Mr. Gowans left England in August last, and has just returned and made a report on the company s entire property as annexed on other side. The directors regret to announce the retirement owing to the pressure of other engage ments of Sir C. E. Lewis, Hart., M.P., as a director of this company. Subsequently the Great Flat Lode was discovered, and has proved to be one of the richest tin-producing lodes of Cornwall. From this lode the rich mines of West Wheal Frances, Wheal Grenville, and South Condurrow fall formerly worked for copper , have paid, and are still paying, large dividends from tin, the profits amounting to in the aggregate to over sfco,000per month. This Great Flat Lode runs through the West Wheal Grenville sett, which in a short distance to the west of South Condurrow on the run of the lode. There can 1 little doubt, had the former workers of Trevoole their efforts and intersected the Great Flat Lode, as the neigh- K nring mines already mentioned have done, Trevoole would have Treen equally productive. The sett is very extensive, having a run of alxnit 800 fathoms on the course of the lode, by alxmt JXK fathoms in width. Three shafts have been sunk to the respective depths of 120 fathoms, 100 fathoms, 50 fathoms, and levels driven intersecting several copper lodes, from which the accounts of the late company show that largo returns were made. The shafts alone represent a very large amount of expenditure. All these shafts are available for the purposes of the present company, and thus a considerable saving of time and expense in development will be effected. The engine shaft has been timbered and secured from service to adit, a distance of 11 fathoms, in a very substantial manner, with skip road, footway, and pumps fixed, shears erected, bwb-pit made, and bob, &c, placed in position. The pumping power is considerable in excess of that of the old works, and ample for all requirements. Pumping has eenlieen started, and the water is now 18 feet below adit. Ground is being cut for bearings, cistern, and for fixing plunger lift preparatory to continuous pumping and the fixing of the permanent work. It is estimated that with a further expenditure of ú8,000 in draining the mine to bottom, fixing pit work, &c, the Great Flat Lode can be proved, when the necessary stamping, crushing, and dressing machinery will have to be provided, for which the remaining capital is considered more tha ample. It must be clearly understood, independent of the prospects for copper, that the chief object of the present company will lie the development of the Great Flnt Lone for tin. The mine is bordered by the main road from Camborne, and is within two miles of Cam borne Station, on the Cornwall Railway. The carriage for coals, Ac which is an important consideration is only Is. The annual report to the 31st July, 1889, submitted to the meeting held on the 29th November, 1889, stated as follows : There are now two distinct mines, viz. : Nottingham Peak, whore a ID-stamp Sandycroft battery has been erected, and is now working ; and Ernlernbo Reef, four miles distant from Nottingham Peak. At both these points a large amount of development has been done and the company twssesses in the Kmlembo Beef a mine of great additional importance. It will bo a matter for almost immediate consideration as to how this new discovery is to be dealt with ; in the meantime a trial crushing of ore from this reef has been ordered to be made at Notting ham Peak battery. Only two prospectors have up to the present been engaged on the concession, with the result that within a few weeks of their going to work, the Emlembo Reef was discovered. Orders have been given to increase the number to fonr. On the 25th September a cablegram was received stating that crushing commenced on the 24th September. During the period, from the 27th July, 1888, to the 24th September, 1888, in addition to the regular mining and developing work, the manager s house, buildings for miners, officers, Ac., were erected, and roads, aerial tram, dam and water race .turbine, &c.t completed, including a large amount of masonry work. When the machinery started it was estimated there wore about 1,200 tons of ore stacked on the surface. Explicit instructions have been given with regard to the sorting of the same so as to ensure an uniform rate of yield. The result of the first crushing , when known, may therefore be accepted as an average of the ore BOfar won. The annual report to the 31st March, 1890, submitted to the meeting held on tie 3rd July, 1890, stated as follows: In March last the directors wrote to Mr. Paull requesting him to send them a report upon ae pxution and prospects of the mine, and to give his opinion as an expert as to whether the indications were sncb as to warrant further expenditure, or, in the alternative, whether it wss lesirsble to discontinue the work and close up the company. He was further asked to supple ment this report by a telegram, to be sent off six weeks after despatch of his letter report, mating whether be had in the interval found reason to alter his opinion or otherwise. ThiB report has duly come to hand, and the directors feel that they cannot do better than lay it l fore the shareholders in eztentn, with the addition of the telegram received from Mr. Paull on the 10th Jnne as requested. At the meeting the directors would desire to obtain from the shareholders an expression of their opinion as to whether the report of Mr. Paull is so far satisfactory as to warrant the continuation of the development therein alluded to. In the Erectors opinion, the indications are distinctly favourable, and so far as they are concerned, a the largest shareholders in the company, they are quite ready to continue the work of such development, and are sanguine as to the result. At meetings on the 29th October and 13th November, 1890, it was resolved to wind up the company voluntarily, and the secretary was appointed liquidator, Ui whom all claims should have been sent by the 28th February, 1891. 96 THE HIKING . The annual report to the 30th June, 1890, submitted to the meeting held on the 30th October, 1890, stated as follows : The Pestarena Mine has been worked with a considerable measure of success, and has, in the quantity of ore produced and gold extracted, shown a marked improvement upon the previous year s operations ; 6,683 tons of ore were treated, yielding 5,112 oss. 2t grs. of gold, or an average of 18 dwts. 8 grs. per ton. The Val Toppa Mine has not developed any ore of importance during the year, and the payable ore available for extraction having become limited, the reduction works were closed between January and April last. On the other concessions belonging to the company sufficient work has been done to maintain the company s rights. The company s indebtedness to the Banca Popolare di Alessandria shows a decrease of ú172 10s. Id. on the year. A considerable sum, however, was diverted from the reduction of this debt, including ú1,200 interest paid to debenture holders, ú163 16s. for stock of timber, and ú141 Is. for purchase of concession Tagliata No. 2. The large sum of ú9,713 appears in thos balance sheet as unpaid debenture interest, and efforts are being made to reduce tuii liability. The general manager s report stated : The ore treated has ljeen mainly obtained from No . 3, 4, and 5 benches, No. 2 bench in the Lady Musgrave tunnel. Lady Norman bench, kipper stones, freehold stopes, and south stopes. Tho best quality of ore treated in the last i Ut of the year has ljeeu from No. 2 bench of the Lady Musgrave tunnel. Nob. 3 and 4 benches have been virtually worked out, the only stone loft there assaying under lodwt. to the ton. No. 5 bench has been worked only to a limited extent. The Lady Norman bench haa beeu n.irked continuously, and the ore from the south-east has been of good quality, but tho further west workings have gone, the ore gradually becoming poorer. The freehold stopes have been worked continuously, and the ore is payable throughout. A rich patch of hematite waa struck in No. 3 floor of these stopes, and some very good kaolin in No. 5 Hoor. The south stope has teen regularly worked, but the quality of the ore has not improved to the extent anticipated -ii months ago, the percentage of silver being occasionally very high and tho yield of gold mjspondingly less. Still there is a large quantity of ore yet to be taken away from these ttopea, which assays from lOdwt. to 3oz. to the ton. Prorpecting operations. Tho Sugarloaf shaft has lieen taken to a depth of1,010 feet. A Inve was put in at the 500 feet level, and another at 1,000 feet. The auriferous stone met with m sinking the shaft at the 350 feet was cut at 500 feet, and rising towards the first. The Linda gully tunnel has beeu considerably extended, but the only discovery of importance waa patches of mundic, kaolin, and soapstone. The directors report submitted on the 12th July, 1893, stated : The directors have felt it desirable, in view of the serious loss attendant on the manufac ture of steel, to suspend work in that department until such time as improved trade permits of resuming it at remunerative prices. The working off of existing contracts has unfortunately fallen as a heavy loss on the year s operations, and but for this and the great depreciation in the value of stocks the results would have been more favourable. The quantitv of coal sold, and which the company have on their books to deliver, is much larger than has been for some time past, and will considerably lessen the cost of production. The cessation for the present of operations in the steel works, combined with other changes in the management, has enabled the directors to effect large economies in the establishment charges, which will be realised in future working, and for these reasons, coupled with the increased output of coal, they are hopeful of being able to submit much more satisfactory accounts of working results at the close of the current year. The directors report, snbmitted in Melbourne on the 26th July, 1894, stated : The report of the refinery works have been a great success. The saving which has been effected is not so apparent in the cost per ton of bullion treated, which has, however, been reduced by 2b. 9Jd. per ton for the reason that tho whole of the bullion when produced is subjected to a preliminary metallurgical operation at the smelters, the result of which is to partially refine the greater portion of it. The balance, which then carries almost the whole of the impurities, is treated at the refinery, whore the process is consequently a more lengthy and costly one ; but this course is more economical than shipping the bullion direct to London in its original state. About 165 tons of antimonitil metal a valuable asset has just been saved, the greater part of which would otherwise have been lost. Work in connection with the extension of the refinery is now well in hand. Several oro developments of importance have been met with, chiefly owing to the open cut proving the existence of bodies of rich oro which might never have been discovered had it not been for the removal of the outcrop. The result of these developments has l cen to mako the weekly yield very irregular, which at times have increased abnormally. Shareholders should therefore not bo alannod when the yield falls below the general average, as it is impossible in the management of a mine of such magnitude to maintain uniformity in the weekly output, if actual yields are to be reported, without incurring considerable expense in handling of the ore. The open cut now practically extends from one end of the mine to the other, and although considerable benefits havealready obtained from the new system, yet so far these have not been appreciable to the fullest extent. A large amount of dead work has had of necossity to be undertaken so that the quarries may be opened systematically, and in such shape as to admit of their being carried down to the depth it is proposed, viz., 300ft. So far, the greatest depth reached is about 100ft. and not more than 30ft. in some portions of the ground. The liquid assets of the company, less outstanding liabilities, amounted to a net sum of ú183,535 4s. Id. The loss through the fall in the price of silver has Iwen considerable, but by the exercise of close and careful super vision expenses have been kept down to the lowest point. The cost of production per ton of ore treated for the half-year has been ú2 12s. Cd. BROKEN HILL SILVER FIELDS, LIMITED. Directors. A. P. Wymond, Chairman. | Charles Clark. | Alexander Kerly. The directors report, submitted on the 21st Jaauary, 1895, stated: With a view to an early distribution of the profits of the company amongst the share holders, and in order to facilitate the development and working of its interests in Matabeleland and Mashonaland, your directors, in conformity with the terms of the land grant obtained from the British South Africa Company, formed the Exploring Land and Minerals Company, Limited, in May last, with a capital of ú250,000. That company acquired for 80,000 fullj-psui shares the whole of the interests of the Exploring Company, other than its shares in the British South Africa Company and in the Bechuanaland Railway Company, Limited. The working capital of ú50,000, which it was stipulated in the land grant should be provided, has been subscribed by the Exploring Company, ami the balance ú120,000 of the capital remains unissued. Of the 80,000 fully-paid shares, 20,000 Bhares are in respect of this company s snaro holding in the Transvaal Minerals, Limited, and its loan to that company. In anticipation of the formation of the Exploring Land and Minerals Company, Mr. C. J. Alford was dispatched to Mashonaland to represent that company s interest , and he has succeeded in securing what Bhould be a very valuable coal prospecting area of 1,600 square miles upon the recently-discovered coalfield on the 8any ati River. Thi coal area lies alxmt 60 miles north-west of Lomagunda, reputed to be one of the richest mining centres in Mashonaland. From Mr. Alford s report, it appears that the country present no physical difficulties to overcome in opening up communication with this coalfield, and with the development of the mining industry and the consequent absorption of timber, this di - covery may turn out of enormous importance to the country and to the Land and Mineimli Company. The actual number of shares in the British South Africa Company received in respect of the shares in the United Concessions Company, Limited, held by the company, was 212,773, and 76,000 further like shares were received in satisfaction of the company s special territorial rights. The directors recommend that after providing for the liabilities of the company, including the share capital, the profits, which are represented by unrealised shares, should he distributed amongst the shareholders without realisation, the directors taking, in like manner, their percentage due as provided by the memorandum and articles of association. On tbi footing, one British South Africa Company s share will be distributed to shareholders at once in respect of each share in the company, and the balance of profits as early as practicable. NEW RIETFONTEIN ESTATE GOLD MINES, LIMITED. Directors. A. Goerz, Chairman. F. Lowrey alternated, Birkenruth . | Hugh Crawford. | R. G. Fricker. Julius Friedlauder alternate A. Friedlander . E. H. Dunning, Managing Director and Life Governor. London Committee. M. Lilienfeld. | Albert Hess. | Harold Smart. General Manager. Consulting Engineer. Secretary. D. E. Doveton. John Hays Hammond. S. B. Dowling. Berlin Agency. Deutsche Treuhaad Gesellschaft, 9, Behrenstrasse, W. Head Offices. London Secretary & Offloes. Johannesburg, South Africa. John Seear, Warnford Court, E.C. This company was formed in January, 1892, to acquire and work the property of the Bu Preez Gold Mining and Estate Company, Limited, consisting of the Da Preez Farm, of about 4,500 acres, and also the mynpacht of Mailer s portion of the Farm Rietfontein, on the Main Reef, Witwatersrand. In July, 1892, the company was reconstructed. The authorised capital is ú160,000, in 160,000 shares of ú1 each ; all the shares have been issued and are fully paid, 125,000 being allotted credited M paid. In July, 1892, 25,000 shares were Bold to the Gold Fields of South Africa, Limited, and A. Goerz representing the Deutsche Bank , jointly, at ú2 per share. Interim dividends of 10 per cent, in January, 1893, and 15 per cent, in July, 1893, were declared j none since. The Du Preez Company was crushing from 1888, to June, 1890. The present 594 . NEW BIETFONTEIN EBTATE. company commenced crashing in February, 1892, with 20 stamps, and 30 more stamps were added in September, 1893, making a total of 50 stamps. Daring 1889, the Da Preez Company obtained 743oz. of gold, and in 1890, 2,547 tons were crushed yielding l,408oz. of gold. For 1892, the yield of gold wasll,555oz. During 1893,24,048 tons were crushed, yielding 28,169o . of gold, and 6,967oz. were obtained from tailings, making a total for the year of 35,126oz. For details of 1894 crushings. see pages 691 to 696. The acconnts are made up annually to the 30th June and submitted in October. The first acoounts, made up to the 31st December, 1892, showed a net working profit of ú20,200 Is. 5d., in addition to which the sum of ú35,000 had been realised by premiums on shares sold, making a credit to profit and loss of ú55,200 Is. 5d.; of this the dividend absorbed ú16,000, and the balance of ú39,200 Is. 5d. was carried forward. At the 30th June, 1893, a credit to profit and loss of ú47,064 19s. 9d. was carried forward, after deducting amount of dividends and directors fees and writing off ú4,578 10s. 6d. for prospecting and mine redemption. The accounts to the 30th June, 1894, showed gross revenue, ú94,865 17s. Id. against expenses and development, ú75,066 lis., and deprecia tion ú17,379 7s. 6d., total ú92,445 18s. 6d, leaving a net profit on the year of ú2,419 18s. 7 1. Adding to this the balance forward made a credit to profit and loss of ú49,484 18s. 4d. ; cash balances, ú6,039 19s. 8d., including ú5,000 on tiled deposit. Transfer form, common i fee, 2s. 6d. per deed. Share-warrants to bearer are issued. Latest price, 2|, 2 . ANNUAL REPORT. The directors report, submitted on the 31st October, 1894. stated : Your directors, in issuing their first annual report, informed you that 40.706 tons of orewere in sight. It has since been discovered tLat a large proportion of this ore has proved un payable, and they have now considered it advisable to write off the whole amount standing to the credit of development account viz., ú22,200 8s. ml. In addition to this amount, 10 per cent, has been written off buildings, 20 per cent, off machinery and plant, and the sum of ú6,009 16s. 9d. off permanent works, making a total of ú39,8.10 16s. 2d., thus leaving all the assets of thecompany standing at very reasonable figures. During the year the management has had to encounter most serious and unforeseen difficulties. In the EaBt and West Mines, from which ore has been taken, serious faults have frequently occurred, caused by the intrusion of diorite and breaks and slides in the formation, and it was at one time a matter of grave con sideration as to the possibility of keeping your mill supplied with payable ore. The loss of the reef in No. 2 shart was the greatest difficulty the board had to contend with, the reef twin cot off at the third level by a longitudinal slide dipping at an angle of 38 degrees, causing a down throw, which has been proved by a cross-cut carried in south on the third level to the Stable Reef without discovering the middle reef. Besides the slide mentioned, a diorite dyke encountered in the third level, cutting off the reef in the stopes going east. The efforts of the management have throughout been centred in the endeavour to locate the faulted reefs in the different parts of the mine, mid to this end prospecting and development work has been pushed forward with all possible speed. The total number of feet driven and sunk during the year amounts to 6,472. Development work has lieen chiefly confined to Nos. 2, 6, and 7 shafts. During the past year, with an average of 33 stamps per month, 29,789 tons of ore have been crushed, yielding 22,797oz. ldwt.,or an average of 15 305dwt. j er ton ; value, ú2 15s. 8 888d. per ton. The board has already informed you of the particulars of a contract entered into with the African fiold Recovery Companj- for the treatment of 100,000 tons of tailings. The total number of tons treated up to date is 30,337, yielding in bullion 7,821oz. 14dwt., or an average of 6166dwt. per ton, the company receiving as its share of the profits ú8,521.

H. Raw. | Arch. Parker. | L. Wagner. London Secretaries. South African Trust and Finance Company, Limited, 1, Crosby Square. E.C. This company was formed in April, 1888, for the purpose of acquiring the Farm Varkensfontein, 5,600 acres in extent, including a mynpacht, situated in the Heidelberg district of the Transvaal. The purchase consideration was ú112,000 in shares. About 400 claims outside the mynpacht have been let to various parties. The company holds 7,750 shares in the Marais Nigel Company. The authorised capital is ú160,000, in 160,000 fully-paid shares of ú1 each. A dividend of 5 per cent, was declared in March, 1889. The next distribution was 10 per cent, in May, 1892, and in June, 1892, 20 per cent, was declared. In December, 1892, a dividend of 17.1 per cent, was announced. The following quarterly dividends have since been paid : Quarter ending March 31st, 1893, 10 per cent. June 30th 15 Sept. 30th 15 Dec. 30th ,, 10 Quarter ending March 31st, 1891, 10 per cent. June 30th 15 Sept. 30th 15 Dec. 31st ., 10 Dividends are paid in London about two months after declaration. Crushing commenced in July, 1888, with 10 stamps, and from April, 1890, up to March, 1891, 20 stamps were working ; crushing was then disfor further development and the erection of a new 25-stamp mill. This was started in September, 1891, and has since been working. Five additional stamps were started in November, 1894, making 30 in all. The tailings are being treated by cyanide process. During the year 1888, l,594oz. of gold were obtained. For 1889, the yield was 3,699oz. In 1890, 6,657 tons were crushed, yielding 13.601oz. of gold. In 1892, 18,030 tons yielded 26,570oz. of gold, and 20,686oz. were obtained from tailings and 3,032oz. from concentrates, making a total for the jear of 50,287oz. During 1893, 22,273 tons were crushed, yielding 25,455oz. of gold, and tailings yielded 17,036oz. and concentrates 3,516oz., making total yield for the year 46,007oz. of gold. For details of 1894 crushings, see pages 691 to 696. The accounts are made up annually to the 30th June, and submitted in July. The accounts to the 30th June, 1893, showed a net profit on the year of ú71,034 7s. 10d., making with the balance forward a credit to profit and loss of ú76,143 12s. 3d. Of this the dividends for the year absorbed ú67,987 5s., and the balance of ú8,156 7s. 3d. was carried forward. The accounts to the 30th June, 1894, showed receipts ú175,004 5s. 4d., and expenditure ú83,006 12s., besides -which ú12,732 2s. 9d. was written off for depreciation, leaving a net profit of ú79.265 10s. 7d. Adding to this the balance forward made an available sum of ú87,421 17s. 10d., of which the dividends absorbed ú79,985, the sum of ú5,000 was placed to reserve, and ú2,436 17s. lOd. carried forward. Transfer form, common ; fee, 2s. 6d. per deed. Shares may be transferred from London to colonial register and vice versa for a fee of 2s. 6d. per certificate. Latest price, 4 ú, o j. ANNUAL REPORT. The directors report, submitted on the 31st July, 1894, stated : It is becoming apparent that the present battery will eventually require to be replaced by more powerful and more modern one. The board anticipate they will be able from time to time to accumulate cash sufficient for this purpose without interfering with the regular declaration of dividends, and out of the above amount have placed ú5,000 to reserve fund. Out of the profits earned during the year the sum of ú3,060 has been expended on buildings, plant, and mine equipment. During the year 23,301 tons of ore have been treated, yielding 47,710oz., giving approximately H, liJNoz. of standard gold, valued at ú170,977 0s. lid. The mine has been worked by twelve incline shafts at a distance of 250ft. apart. The manager proposes to dispense with six of these shafts, and to continue sinking the six remaining as hauling shafts. The manager found on taking over the mine, 18,173 tons of ore reserves, since that date 4,510 tons beyond the quartz cruBhed have been further developed, making a total estimated tonnage of 22,983 tons, which can be reckoned upon as payable rock. The company possesses what it is hoped may prove a valuable asset in 45,000 tons of tailings residues, which have hitherto not been found capable of remunerative treatment. A contract has recently been entered into for the treatment of these residues on payment of a royalty. New ground. Sinking in the shafts south of No. 9 has been proceeded with without .tisfactory result so far. The grade of the ore, however, shows slight improvement as greater depth is being reached. 600 THE MININO . NOOITGEDACHT ESTATE AND GOLD MINING COMPANY, LIMITED. Liquidator. Herbert C. Porter, 8, Old Jewry, E.C. This company was registered on the 8th March, 1892,with a capital of ú160,000 in ú1 shares, being a reconstruction of a company of the same name, registered on the lBt March, 1889, to acquire from the Gold Estates Transvaal Company, Limited, the freehold gold farm Nooitgedacht, 4,K51 acres in extent, situated about Beven miles from Lydcnburg, South African Republic. All the shares were issued and fully paid. The old company paid ú40,000 in cash, and ú95,000 in shares for the property. In 1893, owing to the funds being exhausted, a loan of ú10,000 was obtained from the vendors, the Gold Estates Transvaal Company. In January, 1894, the mine was shut down, operations having been for some months conducted at a loss. On the 5th of March, 1894, resolutions were passed to wind up voluntarily, and transfer the property back to the Gold Estates Company. In consideration that company had agreed to extinguish all liabilities including the ú10,000 loan , and to allot to this company 20,000 fully-paid Gold Estates shares for dis tribution amongst the shareholders, being one for every eight Nooitgedacht shares held. NORTH EASTERN BULTFONTEIN, LIMITED. Liquidator and Receiver. Registered Offices. Frederick Whinuey, 8, Old Jewry, E.C. 19, Finsbury Circus, EX . This company was registered on the 19th March, 1889, to purchase from the London and South African Exploration Company, Limited, the lease of about 200 claims in the Bultfontein Diamond Mine, Griqualand West, South Africa, at a monthly rental of 30s. per claim. The original purchase consideration was ú15,000. The company subsequently acquired additional claims from the same company, and the total number of claims held is 671, acquired at a total coBt of ú68,150. The authorised capital is ú100,100, divided into 100,000 ordinary and 100 founders shares of ú1 each ; all the shares have been issued and fully paid. There are 6 per cent, first mortgage debentures to the amount of ú89,000, of which ú47,000 were issued to the London and South African Exploration Company, Limited, as part payment of purchase consideration, and the balance was subscribed at 86 per cent. At the 30th September, 1892, the accounts showed a debit to revenue account of ú3,090 Is., the year s operations having resulted in a loss of ú4.547 Os. 3d. The diamond production was ú43,739 14s. 6d. for the first half -year, and ú87, 182 0s. 6d. for the second half-year. In consequence of the yield falling off, the company was unable to meet its liabilities. Proceedings were taken by creditors in South Africa, and a receiver and manager on behalf of the debenture-holders was appointed in England is August, 1893. At a meeting held on the 25th September, 1S93, it was resolved to wind np voluntarily ; and on the 27th October, 1893, the voluntory winding up was ordered to continue under the supervision of the Court. A reconstruction scheme was put forward dtiriDg 1894, but not accepted, the local creditors objecting. The Official Liquidator at Kimberley has sold the assets in that district. The consideration for the property was ú7,800, payable ú2,800 in cash and ú5,000 in fully-paid shares. This company was registered on the 9th October, 1898, and is a reconstruction of Spitzkop, Limited. Spitzkop, Limited, was registered on the 20th October, 1880, and was a reconstruction of the New Spitzkop, Limited, registered on the 3rd October, 1887, which was itself a reconstruc tion of the Spitzkop Lydenbnrg Gold Mining Company, Limited, registered on the 25th July, 1883. to purchase the freehold and concession for working the gold mines of the Spitzkop Kstate. consisting of 7,2HO acres, situate in Lydenburg, South Africa. An unsuccessful -attempt having ljeen made in January, 1893, to raise further working capital by the issue of lel entures, resolutions were passed on the 11th and confirmed on the 29th August, 1893, to wind up voluntarily and again reconstruct, the secretary being appointed liquidator. The authorised capital is ú145,000, in 145,000 shares of ú1 each ; 99,070 shares have been issued and are fully paid. The directors report, submitted on the 21st Jaauary, 1895, stated: With a view to an early distribution of the profits of the company amongst the share holders, and in order to facilitate the development and working of its interests in Matabeleland and Mashonaland, your directors, in conformity with the terms of the land grant obtained from the British South Africa Company, formed the Exploring Land and Minerals Company, Limited, in May last, with a capital of ú250,000. That company acquired for 80,000 fullj-psui shares the whole of the interests of the Exploring Company, other than its shares in the British South Africa Company and in the Bechuanaland Railway Company, Limited. The working capital of ú50,000, which it was stipulated in the land grant should be provided, has been subscribed by the Exploring Company, ami the balance ú120,000 of the capital remains unissued. Of the 80,000 fully-paid shares, 20,000 Bhares are in respect of this company s snaro holding in the Transvaal Minerals, Limited, and its loan to that company. In anticipation of the formation of the Exploring Land and Minerals Company, Mr. C. J. Alford was dispatched to Mashonaland to represent that company s interest , and he has succeeded in securing what Bhould be a very valuable coal prospecting area of 1,600 square miles upon the recently-discovered coalfield on the 8any at i River. Thi coal area lies alxmt 60 miles north-west of Lomagunda, reputed to be one of the richest mining centres in Mashonaland. From Mr. Alford s report, it appears that the country present no physical difficulties to overcome in opening up communication with this coalfield, and with the development of the mining industry and the consequent absorption of timber, this di - covery may turn out of enormous importance to the country and to the Land and Mineimli Company. The actual number of shares in the British South Africa Company received in respect of the shares in the United Concessions Company, Limited, held by the company, was 212,773, and 76,000 further like shares were received in satisfaction of the company s special territorial rights. The directors recommend that after providing for the liabilities of the company, including the share capital, the profits, which are represented by unrealised shares, should he distributed amongst the shareholders without realisation, the directors taking, in like manner, their percentage due as provided by the memorandum and articles of association. On tbi footing, one British South Africa Company s share will be distributed to shareholders at once in respect of each share in the company, and the balance of profits as early as practicable. In December, 1894, the company sold its interests in the Inez Reef to the United Rhodesia Gold Fields, Limited. Andrew Moir, 120, Bishopagate Street Within, E.G. This company was formed in 1887, to work nine Main Reef claims on the Farm Torffontein, Witwatersrand. In April, 1888, four claims adjoining, which were owned by the Bijou Syndicate, were acquired by amalgamation; and on the 19th March, 1889, seven additional claims and water-rights, situate to the south of the company s property, were acquired. The company acquired the properties at a cost of ú11,250, payable ú6,000 in cash and ú5,250 in shares. In addition the company has acquired the three South Ferriera clai ms for ú25,000. On the 14th August, 1894, it was resolved to purchase the property of the Wemmer, Ferreira, and Worcester Deep Level Company, consisting of 31 claims on the dip of the Wemmer, Ferreira, and Worcester properties, for 44,000 Ferreira shares. At the same time the company transferred to the Band Mines, Limited, tii claims adjoining the Ferreira water-right in exchange for one deep level claim and ú3,000 cash. The authorised capital is ú90,000, in 90,000 shares of ú1 each; 89,000 shares are issued and fully paid. The capital was increased from ú12,000 to ú28,000 in April. 1888 ; again increased to ú33,000 in March, 1889; and further increased to ú45,000 in December, 1890, by the creation of 12,000 new shares of bleach, of which 11,000 were offered for subscription at ú7 per share to the then shareholders. in the proportion of one new share for every three held, and the remaining 1,000 were sold in October, 1891, at ú8 10s. per share. For 1892 the yield was 35,342oz. from 49,250 tons crushed, besides 12,891oz. of gold obtained from treatment of tailings, making total yield for the year of 48,233oz. Supplemen tary accounts made up to the 30th June, 1894, and submitted on the 14th August, 1894, showed a net profit for the three months of ú36,196 11b. 5d., making a total credit to profit and loss of ú207,411 6s. 7d. This company was formed in January, 1892, to acquire and work the property of the Bu Preez Gold Mining and Estate Company, Limited, consisting of the Da Preez Farm, of about 4,500 acres, and also the mynpacht of Mailer s portion of the Farm Rietfontein, on the Main Reef, Witwatersrand. In July, 1892, the company was reconstructed. The authorised capital is ú160,000, in 160,000 shares of ú1 each ; all the shares have been issued and are fully paid, 125,000 being allotted credited M paid. In July, 1892, 25,000 shares were Bold to the Gold Fields of South Africa, Limited, and A. Goerz representing the Deutsche Bank , jointly, at ú2 per share. Interim dividends of 10 per cent, in January, 1893, and 15 per cent, in July, 1893, were declared j none since. The Du Preez Company was crushing from 1888, to June, 1890. The present 594 . NEW BIETFONTEIN EBTATE. company commenced crashing in February, 1892, with 20 stamps, and 30 more stamps were added in September, 1893, making a total of 50 stamps. Daring 1889, the Da Preez Company obtained 743oz. of gold, and in 1890, 2,547 tons were crushed yielding l,408oz. of gold. For 1892, the yield of gold wasll,555oz. During 1893,24,048 tons were crushed, yielding 28,169o . of gold, and 6,967oz. were obtained from tailings, making a total for the year of 35,126oz. For details of 1894 crushings. see pages 691 to 696. The acconnts are made up annually to the 30th June and submitted in October. The first acoounts, made up to the 31st December, 1892, showed a net working profit of ú20,200 Is. 5d., in addition to which the sum of ú35,000 had been realised by premiums on shares sold, making a credit to profit and loss of ú55,200 Is. 5d.; of this the dividend absorbed ú16,000, and the balance of ú39,200 Is. 5d. was carried forward. At the 30th June, 1893, a credit to profit and loss of ú47,064 19s. 9d. was carried forward, after deducting amount of dividends and directors fees and writing off ú4,578 10s. 6d. for prospecting and mine redemption. The accounts to the 30th June, 1894, showed gross revenue, ú94,865 17s. Id. against expenses and development, ú75,066 lis., and deprecia tion ú17,379 7s. 6d., total ú92,445 18s. 6d, leaving a net profit on the year of ú2,419 18s. 7 1. Adding to this the balance forward made a credit to profit and loss of ú49,484 18s. 4d. ; cash balances, ú6,039 19s. 8d., including ú5,000 on tiled deposit. Transfer form, common i fee, 2s. 6d. per deed. Share-warrants to bearer are issued. Latest price, 2|, 2 . ANNUAL REPORT. The directors report, submitted on the 31st October, 1894. stated : Your directors, in issuing their first annual report, informed you that 40.706 tons of orewere in sight. It has since been discovered tLat a large proportion of this ore has proved un payable, and they have now considered it advisable to write off the whole amount standing to the credit of development account viz., ú22,200 8s. ml. In addition to this amount, 10 per cent, has been written off buildings, 20 per cent, off machinery and plant, and the sum of ú6,009 16s. 9d. off permanent works, making a total of ú39,8.10 16s. 2d., thus leaving all the assets of thecompany standing at very reasonable figures. During the year the management has had to encounter most serious and unforeseen difficulties. In the EaBt and West Mines, from which ore has been taken, serious faults have frequently occurred, caused by the intrusion of diorite and breaks and slides in the formation, and it was at one time a matter of grave con sideration as to the possibility of keeping your mill supplied with payable ore. The loss of the reef in No. 2 shart was the greatest difficulty the board had to contend with, the reef twin cot off at the third level by a longitudinal slide dipping at an angle of 38 degrees, causing a down throw, which has been proved by a cross-cut carried in south on the third level to the Stable Reef without discovering the middle reef. Besides the slide mentioned, a diorite dyke encountered in the third level, cutting off the reef in the stopes going east. The efforts of the management have throughout been centred in the endeavour to locate the faulted reefs in the different parts of the mine, mid to this end prospecting and development work has been pushed forward with all possible speed. The total number of feet driven and sunk during the year amounts to 6,472. Development work has lieen chiefly confined to Nos. 2, 6, and 7 shafts. During the past year, with an average of 33 stamps per month, 29,789 tons of ore have been crushed, yielding 22,797oz. ldwt.,or an average of 15 305dwt. j er ton ; value, ú2 15s. 8 888d. per ton. The board has already informed you of the particulars of a contract entered into with the African fiold Recovery Companj- for the treatment of 100,000 tons of tailings. The total number of tons treated up to date is 30,337, yielding in bullion 7,821oz. 14dwt., or an average of 6166dwt. per ton, the company receiving as its share of the profits ú8,521.

This company was formed in April, 1888, for the purpose of acquiring the Farm Varkensfontein, 5,600 acres in extent, including a mynpacht, situated in the Heidelberg district of the Transvaal. The purchase consideration was ú112,000 in shares. About 400 claims outside the mynpacht have been let to various parties. The company holds 7,750 shares in the Marais Nigel Company. The authorised capital is ú160,000, in 160,000 fully-paid shares of ú1 each. A dividend of 5 per cent, was declared in March, 1889. The next distribution was 10 per cent, in May, 1892, and in June, 1892, 20 per cent, was declared. In December, 1892, a dividend of 17.1 per cent, was announced. The following quarterly dividends have since been paid : Quarter ending March 31st, 1893, 10 per cent. June 30th 15 Sept. 30th 15 Dec. 30th ,, 10 Quarter ending March 31st, 1891, 10 per cent. June 30th 15 Sept. 30th 15 Dec. 31st ., 10 Dividends are paid in London about two months after declaration. Crushing commenced in July, 1888, with 10 stamps, and from April, 1890, up to March, 1891, 20 stamps were working ; crushing was then disfor further development and the erection of a new 25-stamp mill. This was started in September, 1891, and has since been working. Five additional stamps were started in November, 1894, making 30 in all. The tailings are being treated by cyanide process. During the year 1888, l,594oz. of gold were obtained. For 1889, the yield was 3,699oz. In 1890, 6,657 tons were crushed, yielding 13.601oz. of gold. In 1892, 18,030 tons yielded 26,570oz. of gold, and 20,686oz. were obtained from tailings and 3,032oz. from concentrates, making a total for the jear of 50,287oz. During 1893, 22,273 tons were crushed, yielding 25,455oz. of gold, and tailings yielded 17,036oz. and concentrates 3,516oz., making total yield for the year 46,007oz. of gold. For details of 1894 crushings, see pages 691 to 696. The accounts are made up annually to the 30th June, and submitted in July. The accounts to the 30th June, 1893, showed a net profit on the year of ú71,034 7s. 10d., making with the balance forward a credit to profit and loss of ú76,143 12s. 3d. Of this the dividends for the year absorbed ú67,987 5s., and the balance of ú8,156 7s. 3d. was carried forward. The accounts to the 30th June, 1894, showed receipts ú175,004 5s. 4d., and expenditure ú83,006 12s., besides -which ú12,732 2s. 9d. was written off for depreciation, leaving a net profit of ú79.265 10s. 7d. Adding to this the balance forward made an available sum of ú87,421 17s. 10d., of which the dividends absorbed ú79,985, the sum of ú5,000 was placed to reserve, and ú2,436 17s. lOd. carried forward. Transfer form, common ; fee, 2s. 6d. per deed. Shares may be transferred from London to colonial register and vice versa for a fee of 2s. 6d. per certificate. Latest price, 4 ú, o j. ANNUAL REPORT. The directors report, submitted on the 31st July, 1894, stated : It is becoming apparent that the present battery will eventually require to be replaced by more powerful and more modern one. The board anticipate they will be able from time to time to accumulate cash sufficient for this purpose without interfering with the regular declaration of dividends, and out of the above amount have placed ú5,000 to reserve fund. Out of the profits earned during the year the sum of ú3,060 has been expended on buildings, plant, and mine equipment. During the year 23,301 tons of ore have been treated, yielding 47,710oz., giving approximately H, liJNoz. of standard gold, valued at ú170,977 0s. lid. The mine has been worked by twelve incline shafts at a distance of 250ft. apart. The manager proposes to dispense with six of these shafts, and to continue sinking the six remaining as hauling shafts. The manager found on taking over the mine, 18,173 tons of ore reserves, since that date 4,510 tons beyond the quartz cruBhed have been further developed, making a total estimated tonnage of 22,983 tons, which can be reckoned upon as payable rock. The company possesses what it is hoped may prove a valuable asset in 45,000 tons of tailings residues, which have hitherto not been found capable of remunerative treatment. A contract has recently been entered into for the treatment of these residues on payment of a royalty. New ground. Sinking in the shafts south of No. 9 has been proceeded with without .tisfactory result so far. The grade of the ore, however, shows slight improvement as greater depth is being reached. 600 THE MININO . NOOITGEDACHT ESTATE AND GOLD MINING COMPANY, LIMITED. Liquidator. Herbert C. Porter, 8, Old Jewry, E.C. This company was registered on the 8th March, 1892,with a capital of ú160,000 in ú1 shares, being a reconstruction of a company of the same name, registered on the lBt March, 1889, to acquire from the Gold Estates Transvaal Company, Limited, the freehold gold farm Nooitgedacht, 4,K51 acres in extent, situated about Beven miles from Lydcnburg, South African Republic. All the shares were issued and fully paid. The old company paid ú40,000 in cash, and ú95,000 in shares for the property. In 1893, owing to the funds being exhausted, a loan of ú10,000 was obtained from the vendors, the Gold Estates Transvaal Company. In January, 1894, the mine was shut down, operations having been for some months conducted at a loss. On the 5th of March, 1894, resolutions were passed to wind up voluntarily, and transfer the property back to the Gold Estates Company. In consideration that company had agreed to extinguish all liabilities including the ú10,000 loan , and to allot to this company 20,000 fully-paid Gold Estates shares for dis tribution amongst the shareholders, being one for every eight Nooitgedacht shares held. NORTH EASTERN BULTFONTEIN, LIMITED. Liquidator and Receiver. Registered Offices. Frederick Whinuey, 8, Old Jewry, E.C. 19, Finsbury Circus, EX . This company was registered on the 19th March, 1889, to purchase from the London and South African Exploration Company, Limited, the lease of about 200 claims in the Bultfontein Diamond Mine, Griqualand West, South Africa, at a monthly rental of 30s. per claim. The original purchase consideration was ú15,000. The company subsequently acquired additional claims from the same company, and the total number of claims held is 671, acquired at a total coBt of ú68,150. The authorised capital is ú100,100, divided into 100,000 ordinary and 100 founders shares of ú1 each ; all the shares have been issued and fully paid. There are 6 per cent, first mortgage debentures to the amount of ú89,000, of which ú47,000 were issued to the London and South African Exploration Company, Limited, as part payment of purchase consideration, and the balance was subscribed at 86 per cent. At the 30th September, 1892, the accounts showed a debit to revenue account of ú3,090 Is., the year s operations having resulted in a loss of ú4.547 Os. 3d. The diamond production was ú43,739 14s. 6d. for the first half -year, and ú87, 182 0s. 6d. for the second half-year. In consequence of the yield falling off, the company was unable to meet its liabilities. Proceedings were taken by creditors in South Africa, and a receiver and manager on behalf of the debenture-holders was appointed in England is August, 1893. At a meeting held on the 25th September, 1S93, it was resolved to wind np voluntarily ; and on the 27th October, 1893, the voluntory winding up was ordered to continue under the supervision of the Court. A reconstruction scheme was put forward dtiriDg 1894, but not accepted, the local creditors objecting. The Official Liquidator at Kimberley has sold the assets in that district. Tsla company was registered on January 21st, 1882, to acquire the Adjsh Bippo gold-mining property, about 1,800 acres in extent, situate in the Wassau District, Gold Coast Colony, West Africa. Crocker, Swauzy and Cleaver who were the principal vendors to this company , and handed over gratis. The reef-bearing portion of the property is equal to about 2,133 mining claims, and under agreements made with the Consolidated Gold Fields of South Africa, Limited, and South African Gold Trust, Limited, in March, 1900, the New Gold Coast Agency, Limited, is dealing with certain portions; in any subsidiary companies formed the Wossnn Company receives practically half the vendors shares and has the right to subscribe for half the working capital. The Adjah Bippo Deep, Limited, and Cinnamon Bippo, Limited, have been formed by the New Gold Coast Agency, Limited. The company retains and is working the Adjah Bippo Mine, equal to about 166 claims. This company was registered December 13th, 1906, and is a reconstruction of a company of similar name registered October 3rd, 1894 see vol. for 1906 . The properties comprise 175,743 acres freehold land, with mineral rights, subject to royalty, and 1,220,842 acres pastoral leasehold land, situate near Ooolgardie, Western Australia. A block of 18,800 acres was sold by the former company to the Block 45 Hampton Plains Estate, Ltd., and another portion, known as Block 42, G6 . HAMPTON PLAINS ESTATE 1906 - sontiiiued. to the Hampton Gold Fields, Ltd. Several other blocks of freehold land have been sold to various companies ; the extent of freehold land now held is as above. The company also holds a share interest in Victorian Deep Lead gold mining properties and in the Bed Hill company. In 1895 certain rights were sold to the Coolgardie Waterworks, Ltd. see vol. for 1900 , but in January, 1901, it was decided to purchase the undertaking of that company and the waterworks is now owned by this company, but the revenue has almost ceased owing to Government supply; the company has also brickworks, township, Ac. Mining operations were formerly chiefly confined to the Merry Hampton and Queen Mines, but results were disappointing. The Lady Millar Mine at Norseman is being developed, and crushing commenced September, 1907. The company has acquired from the Hampton Properties, Ltd., the lease for 7 years of Block 50, with an option to purchase the whole or part. On reconstruction the oompany held 27,009 fullypaid shares in Bed Hill, Westralia, Gold Minos, Ltd., 69,447 in Block 42, 90,313 in Hampton Properties, Ltd., and sundry other shares. The authorised capital is ú200,000, in 400,000 shares of 10s. each ; 232,083 shares ú116,042 10s. are issued and 9s. paid. There is ú99,560 de benture stock issued in exchange for debenture stock in old company, with rights as below : Under the soheme of reconstruction shareholders were offered one share oredited as 7s. 6d. paid for each fully-paid 16s. share in old oompany, the liability being payable 6d. on application, 6d. on March 31st, 1907, and the balance in calls of 6d. at intervals of not less than six months. The debenture stock bears interest at four per cent, per annum, payable until November 30th, 1910 out of the revenue to be obtained from assets taken over from the old company, but in any year when the profits are suffloient to pay ten per cent, on the share capital the interest is to be six per cent. The stock is redeemable at par by annual drawings of ú5,000 commencing January, 1912, and the proceeds of the sale of any of the assets are to be applied towards redemption ; holders have the right to convert into fully -paid shares at par at any time. Interest payable June 30th and December 30th. The statutory report was submitted March 12th, 1907. Transfer form, common ; fee, 2s. 6d. This company was registered in Melbourne, Victoria, on August 11th, 1903, to take over the undertakings of a former company of same name and the North Mount Lyell Copper Co., Ltd. The former Mount Lyell Mining and Railway Co., Ltd., was registered in Melbourne, Victoria, in 1898, being a reconstruction of a former company of same name formed to ac quire copper mining leases comprising 333 aoree situate at Mount Lyell, about 18 miles from. Macquarie Harbour, on the west coast of Tasmania, also 43 acres for reduction works site. The ore of the mines consists of a mixture of iron and copper pyrites with silver and gold. A special lease for 30 years was granted by the Tasmania Legislature in September, 1893. The company constructed a railway from the mines to Macquarie Harbour. In August, 1900, the adjoining property of the South Tharsis Mining Co., No Liability, was purchased for ú45,000 cash; the adjoining Royal Tharsis Mine, area 40 acres, was also acquired at a total cost of ú13,347, and the King Lyell Mine at a cost of ú2,000. In March, 1902, M.L. Reserve leases and Gleo Lyell leases, and in September, 1902, North Crown Lyell Block No. 602-93 06 acres and Central Lyell Block 47G 10 acres were also acquired. The North Mount Lyell Copper Co., Ltd., was registered on 20th September, 1897, to acquire the property of the North Mount Lyell Mining Co., No Liability, of Melbourne vol. for 1002 , consisting of 80 acres of mining land, in three contiguous blocks of 10 acres each, situate in the northern portion of the Mount Lyell field, Tasmania, and held under mining leases from the Crown; also the right to construct a railway 28 miles long from the mines to Macquarie Harbour. The chief product is copper, but the mine also yields gold and silver. The purchase consideration was ú305,000, in 395,000 fully-paid shares. The railway wbb constructed, and wharf, pier and jetty at Macquarie Harbour completed. The company also erected smelting works. The adjoining property of the Lyell Tharsis Mining Co. see vol. for 1905 has been purchased for ú2,000, and the company now owns 4,108 acres in all. Smelting was commenced by former company in June, 1896, and 11 furnaces are now at work, also plant for treating converter matte. The company has also erected works at Yarraville for the manufacture of superphosphates, and a site has also been secured at Port Adelaide, 8. Australia. For half-year to September 30th, 1906, 199,289 tons treated produced 4,491 tons blister copper, containing 4,444 tons copper, 829,188ozs. silver and 10,746ozs. gold ; costs 15s. 10 4d. per ton. For half-year to March 3 1st, 1907, 206,741 tons produced 3,839 tons blister copper, containing 3,796 tons copper, 355,372ozs. silver and 10,115ozs. gold ; costs 14s. 4-9d. per ton. For half-year to September 30th, 1907, 213,275 tons produced 4,148 tons blister copper, containing 4,090 tons copper, 344,715 ozs. silver and 9,334oxs. gold i costs 15s. 5 2d. per ton. The purchase consideration was ú136,000 in fullypaid shares for the properties and ú22,500 cash for the machinery erected on the Leicester Mine by the former owners. The lease of the Leicester Mine is held from the Cape Government at an annual rental of ú672, with a royalty of 1 per cent, on gross output of diamonds, one-half of the rent and royalty being refunded to the company as owners of the freehold. The former owners, working in 1896, obtained 9,410 carats of diamonds, valued at ú17,218, from Leicester Mine. The Company receives rental from the estate. This company was registered on February 16th, 1895, under the title of the Northern Territories B.S.A. Exploring Co., Ltd., to acquire a concession in BhodeBia granted by the British South Africa Co. conveying the right to locate 400 square miles of land north of the Biver Zambesi carrying ordinary mineral and land rights, together with 12,000 acres of coal area, north or south of the Biver Zambesi, less one-eighth interest held by other parties ; also 100 square miles of ordinary mineral and land rights, less one half interest held by other parties, and 220 gold-mining claims in the Umniati and Quay districts. The purchase con sideration was ú2,000 cash and 71,500 fully-paid shares, and the interests of other parties were purchased in 1899 for 23,984 fully-paid shares. The company s expedition having discovered copper mines on the concession, the name was changed to the above in June, 1899 ; the oompany received a farther grant of 10 square miles, also a further coal area of 12,000 acres. A portion of the coal area has been sold for shares of the Wankie Rhodesia Co. The oompany has also sold a considerable portion of its original properties for 250,000 shares of the Rhodesia Copper Co., Ltd. Certain areas havo been sold to the Kafue Copper Development Co., Ltd., for 150,000 fully-paid shares. The company also holds shares in the Rhodesia Broken Hill Development Co., Ltd. The properties now held consist of mineral and surface rights over three blocks of ten square miles each, mineral rights over ten square miles at Chanobi and ten square miles round each series of sulphur springs near a N Kala and 6 the junction of the Lunga and Kafue rivers, and a ten per cent, interest in certain farms and claims pegged for the Rhodesia Copper Co., Ltd. ; also the right to peg out 500 reef claims and 300 alluvial claims in Northern Rhodesia, of which 275 claims had been located at June 30th, 1907. Prospecting is being carried on in conjunction with the Rhodesia Copper Co., Ltd. The authorised capital is ú250,000, in 250,000 shares of ú1 each ; 204,630 shares are issued and fully paid. There are debentures as below. An issue of ú100,000 six per cent, debentures was made in April, 1903, redeemable 31st Dec, 1908, at 105, unless previously converted into shares at holder s option at ú10 per share ; each subscriber of ú100 debentures received the right up till December 31st, 1907, of taking up at any time 10 shares of the reserve capital at ú10 per share ; the issue was guaranteed on five per cent, cash commission, and an option on 20,000 shares up to December 3lBt, 1908, at ú10, the guarantors being the Charter Trust and Agenoy, Ltd., the South African Gold Trust, Ltd., and the Bechuanaland Exploration Co., Ltd. The agency business carried on by the Gold Fields of Matabeleland, Ltd., in Bhodesia, has also been acquired. In 1903, the assets of the Filabusi Charterland Goldfields, Ltd., were pnrchased for 1,000 shares. In December, 1906, it was deoided to extend the company s operations outside Africa. This company was registered June 22nd, 1894, and is a reconstruction of the Van Ryn Estate and Gold Mining Co., Ltd. see vol. for 1896 . The property now owned is situated on the farm Vlakfontein No. 442, in the Eastern district of the Witwatersrand Gold Fields, and is 7,944 acres in extent, in addition to an unproclaimed area of 534 morgen recently acquired. The mining area on the Van Eyn reef, the ore body which is being worked, consists of some 136 intact reef claims after allowing for the ground exhausted, while the company s waterrights cover 351$ acres. Crushing commenced in February, 1892, and in October, 1896, a new 80-stamp mill and cyanide plant were started. Another 80-stamp mill was acquired with the Van Ryn West property, making 160 stamps. A new slimes plant has been added and four tube mills are being erected. Crushing was suspended in October, 1899, and during the war the machinery was damaged by the Boers, but this has been replaced, and crushing was resumed in June, 1903. This company was registered in the Transvaal on the 29th November, 1886, to acquire the farm Driefontein, Eastern Witwatersrand, 10,355 acres in extent, with a mynpacht of 300 claims. The vendors received ú45,000 in cash and ú150,000 in shares. In January, 1889, the company sold 36 claims for ú210,000 in cash and ú15,000 in shares of the Balmoral Main Reef Gold Mining Co., Ltd. In May, 1893, 12 claims adjoining the Glencairn property, with the corresponding 48 dip claims, making 60 in all, were sold to Barnato Bros, for ú60,000. The company now owns Mynpacht No. 320, 296 morgen in extent, 78 prospecting and two diggers claims and 4,780 morgen freehold on farm Drietfontein, No. 148, Boksburg district, Transvaal, and also derives a revenue of about ú11,000 per annum from a residential estate of 4,892 acres, situate on the said farm to the north of the main reef series. Crushing commenced in January, 1888, with a 50-stamp mill, and further 50 stamps were afterwards added, but crushing was suspended in 1891 and the mine has since been thoroughly developed and a new 120-stamp mill and cyanide plant erected, crushing being resumed in 1896. A further 100 stamps making 220 and slimes plant have been added and are now at work. This company was registered in the Transvaal in April, 1894, and is a recon struction of the Kleinfontein Estate and Gold Mining Co., Ltd. see vol. for 1895 . Including 92 dip claims purchased in 1895 from the Benoni Claim Synd., for 00, XXJ fully-paid shares, the property comprised 151 claims on the Farms Klein fontein and Benoni, Eastern Witwatersrand. In April, 1902, the undertaking of the Kleinfontein Central Gold Mines, Ltd. registered September, 1895, see vol. for 1903 , was purchased for 225,000 fully-paid shares ; its property comprised 103 claims. In 1903, 20 south-western claims were exchanged for seven claims on the east and 10,295 shares of the Kleinfontein Deep, Ltd. since sold . The company now owns 299 claims, on Farms Benoni and Kleinfontein, west of Modderfontein and Geduld, East Rand, Transvaal. Crnshing commenced in May, 1894 ; a new mill of 140 stamps was afterwards erected. Operations wore suspended in October, 1899, owing to the war; in December, 1900, the Boers destroyed the entire mill, crusher station, &c. A new mill of 220 stamps and cyanide :md slimes works arc now erected ; crushing was resumei . in October, 1904. During 1907,389,221 tons yielded gold value ú63-4.434; profit .C230.804 or lis. 10 3d. per ton, an increase of 2a. 6 7d. per ton over 1908: During 1908, 150,791 tons yielded ú677,130; estimated profit ú271,603; costs at December, 10s. lOd. per ton. For crushings see table at commencement of . 399 NEW KLEINFONTEIIT volume. Ore reserves, at December, 1907, 949,434 tons value 8 3 dwts. per ton ; at September, 1908, 1,041,016 tons. The authorised capital is ú900,000, in 900,000 shares ofúl each; all the shares are issued and folly paid. The capital was formerly ú110,000, and shareholders received one fully-paid New Kleinfontein share for every three Kleinfontein Kstate shares ; in 1895, it was inoreased to ú185,000, and 15,000 shares were offered to shareholders at ú4 each, the issue being guaranteed ; inoreased in 1896 to ú250,000, when 46,250 shares were offered to shareholders at ú3 ; increased in 1898 to ú275,000, and in 1902 increased to ú800,000, 225,000 fully -paid shares being issued in payment for Kleinfontein Central undertaking ; 228,124 offered to shareholders of both companies at 32s. 6d. each, the guarantors, the Anglo-French Exploration Co., Ltd., subscribing for a further 60,000 at 32s. 6d. each, and 24,661 fully-paid issued in settlement of a loan of ú10,000 ; in 1904, 31,013 shares wore sold to the Anglo-French Co. at 37s. 6d. each. In May, 1905, the capital was increased to present amount, the 100,000 new shares being offered to shareholders at 42s. 6d. ; the issue was guaranteed by the Anglo-French Exploration Co., Ltd., and others, at same price, for a commission of 5 per cent.
